claims.claim: When a level-1 warning was issued and overcharging ceased, the maximum battery temperature reached only 28.4°C, no pressure change was observed, and no damage occurred to the battery.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature of only 28.4 ◦C indicates that the reaction heat produced inside the battery remains normal at this stage. No change was observed in the pressure parameters during the entire process and no damage occurred to the battery.

claims.claim: When a level-2 warning was issued and overcharging ceased, the maximum battery temperature reached 41.5°C, and the pressure parameters returned to normal after the first surge, indicating minimal battery deformation with subsequent automatic recovery.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature was higher than that associated with the first-level warning at 41.5 ◦C, indicating that the reaction heat production inside the battery had begun to intensify, but it was still controllable. As the overcharging ceased, the pressure parameters began to decrease gradually after the first surge and slowly returned to normal, indicating minimal battery deformation with subsequent automatic recovery.

claims.claim: When a level-4 warning was issued and overcharging ceased, thermal runaway could not be prevented, and the maximum battery temperature reached 705.2°C.
claims.quote: When the pressure spikes for the second time; that is, when a level-four warning is issued, overcharging ceases and the subsequent battery voltage drops abruptly. However, after a short period, the battery experienced thermal runaway, with an immediate voltage drop to zero. The temperature continues to rise after overcharging ceases until thermal runaway occurs, and the maximum temperature reaches 705.2 ◦C.

claims.claim: The first characteristic pressure parameter (Pc1) occurs at approximately 9.75% SOC before thermal runaway, and the third characteristic pressure (Pc3) occurs at approximately 2.53% SOC before thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The occurrence time of the second group of characteristic parameters was approximately 10 % SOC before thermal runaway. Pc1 was used as the warning parameter at this point... The fourth group of characteristic parameters appeared at approximately 2.5 % SOC. Pc3 was used as the warning parameter

claims.claim: The escape time prediction errors for all four warning levels were within 20 seconds, with level-1 error at 16.56 s, level-2 at 11.52 s, level-3 at 11.88 s, and level-4 at 10.08 s.
claims.quote: The escape time errors were 16.56 s, 11.52 s, and 11.88 s respectively, all within 20 s for each level.

claims.claim: At a bending angle of 60° in a fully charged state (100% SOC), thermal runaway is triggered in LiCoO2/graphite soft pack LIBs under three-point bending.
claims.quote: it was found that 60◦ is the critical angle for the TR of LIBs in a fully charged state

claims.claim: At 30% SOC and a 60° bending angle, thermal runaway did not occur and the temperature did not rise significantly.
claims.quote: When the SOC was in the 30% state, TR did not occur, and the temperature did not rise significantly.

claims.claim: With decreasing SOC at a fixed 60° bending angle, the time to thermal runaway increases and the maximum temperature of thermal runaway decreases, with the highest temperature at 40% SOC reaching only 248°C.
claims.quote: With the gradual decrease in the battery SOC, the time of the battery thermal runaway gradually increases, the maximum temperature of the battery thermal runaway gradually decreases, and the highest temperature of the battery with 40% SOC is only 248℃.

claims.claim: At a critical bending angle of approximately 135°, there exists a competing relationship where below this angle irreversible heat dominates, and above this angle ISC Joule heat becomes the primary heat source and total heat generated exceeds that of normal batteries.
claims.quote: When the bending degree falls below this critical point, the internal resistance of the battery increases, with irreversible heat playing a predominant role in heat generation. Conversely, as the bending degree surpasses this critical point, the battery's ISC resistance (RISC) decreases, and heat generation from ISC becomes the primary contributor to the overall heat produced by the battery.

claims.claim: When ISC occurs due to bending, the solid-phase ion diffusion coefficient Ds decreases significantly by more than 80%, because rapid electron movement through the collector generates Joule heat and electrolytes cannot transport ions to the electrode surface in time.
claims.quote: When the bending degree continues to increase, the ion diffusion rate will significantly decrease above 80%. This is due to the rapid movement of electrons through the collector between the positive and negative electrodes when an ISC occurs, generating a large amount of Joule heat. Electrolytes are unable to transport ions to the electrode surface in time to participate in the electrochemical reaction.

claims.claim: Under external heating alone, thermal runaway of LiFeO4 battery occurs but combustion does not occur, as flammable and explosive gases are not ignited without an ignition source.
claims.quote: it can be concluded that thermal runaway of LiFeO4 battery used in this study does not cause combustion. Although there are lots of gases being generated, the flammable and explosive gases are not ignited without ignition sources.

claims.claim: Under external heating-ESC coupling abuse, the high-temperature electric conductor (temperature above 1000°C due to Joule heat) acts as the ignition source for flammable and explosive gases after the safety valve opens.
claims.quote: The temperature of the electric conductor is higher than 1000°C owing to Joule heat. The electric conductor is seen as ignition source. The explosive gas mixture is ignited.

claims.claim: External heating contributes much more to the accumulated heat of the battery before safety valve opening compared to the external short circuit under external heating-ESC coupling abuse.
claims.quote: The external heating contributes much more to accumulated heat of battery at the moment of safety valve open. This means that external heating plays the key role in the thermal runaway and combustion of battery under external heating-ESC coupling abuse.

claims.claim: With increasing external resistance, the maximum discharging current decreases, the combustion duration decreases and is less violent, but the exothermic side reactions increase after thermal runaway.
claims.quote: With the increase of external resistance, the combustion lasts shorter and is less violent, but the exothermic side reactions increase after thermal runaway.

claims.claim: With increasing triggering time of ESC, the combustion duration increases but the exothermic side reactions decrease after thermal runaway.
claims.quote: With the increase of triggering time of ESC, the combustion lasts longer, but the exothermic side reactions decrease after thermal runaway.
